analysis: Stronger designs converge on three structural ideas: (1) aggressively reduce candidates (skyline / nondominated pruning) to cut N; (2) avoid repeated exact HV calls by approximating contributions with a single shared Monte‑Carlo sample set and precomputed boolean domination masks (computed in memory‑safe blocks); (3) select greedily with incremental bookkeeping (covered sample counts, lazy max‑heap or direct subtraction) and then perform a bounded exact‑HV local swap on a small shortlist. Good heuristics reuse data (samples, masks, box‑vol tie breakers), vectorize mask arithmetic, and limit exact HV only to the polishing phase. Weaker implementations recompute HVs inside tight loops, recreate heavy objects repeatedly, iterate naively over Python sets/lists, or forget pruning and mask reuse; these incur excessive CPU and memory overhead. Practical implementation notes seen across codes: blockwise mask building prevents OOM, lazy heap avoids full recomputation of all marginal gains each selection, incremental update of contribution counts via mask subtraction is far cheaper than recomputing from scratch, and mapping between pruned and global indices must be maintained to return correct points. Lastly, small deterministic RNGs and caps on sample size (scaled with k and D) balance accuracy and runtime.
experience: Prune dominated points, reuse one shared MC sample set built in blocks, precompute boolean domination masks, select with a lazy greedy or incremental-count strategy, then do an exact‑HV polish on a tiny shortlist. Avoid exact HV inside inner loops and recreate heavy objects; vectorize mask ops and cap sample size proportional to k and D.
current_reflection: Prune dominated points, reuse one shared MC sample set built in blocks, precompute boolean domination masks, select with a lazy greedy or incremental-count strategy, then do an exact‑HV polish on a tiny shortlist. Avoid exact HV inside inner loops and recreate heavy objects; vectorize mask ops and cap sample size proportional to k and D.
bad_reflections: Focus on data reuse and vectorized updates. Use shared samples and lazy evaluations for marginal gains while combining aggressive candidate pruning with local refinements to improve both accuracy and runtime.

Focus on aggressive pruning and vectorized, lazy-updating of contribution estimates. Reuse sampled data for both approximation and local search. Integrate global greedy selection with a focused exact-swap phase on a shortlist to cut overhead while still polishing hypervolume performance.

Focus on reusing intermediate data (e.g., pre-computed masks and samples), implement lazy updates to avoid full re-evaluations, and incorporate quick filtering (like Pareto/pruning) to reduce candidate space for subsequent precise refinements.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: prune dominated; shared block MC samples; precomputed boolean domination masks; lazy greedy / incremental counts; vectorized mask ops; shortlist exact HV polish; sample size ∝ k·D.
- Advice: Build one blockwise MC sample set, precompute domination masks, aggressively prune candidates, update marginal gains with lazy greedy or incremental counts using vectorized mask ops, cap sample size ~O(k·D), then run exact hypervolume only on a tiny shortlisted set.
- Avoid: exact HV inside inner loops; recreating heavy objects each iteration; full reevaluation of all candidates; oversized sample sets.
- Explanation: Reuse and vectorize intermediates to minimize recomputation; lazy updates and pruning shrink work; final exact polish recovers precision cheaply.
